context:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Syllabus:
6.1200 Mathematics for Computer Science

Course Meeting Times:
- Lectures: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session
- Recitation: 2 sessions/week; 1 hour/session

Prerequisites:
The only prerequisite is 18.01 Single Variable Calculus. If you have already taken 18.200 Principles of Discrete Applied Mathematics or 6.1220 Design and Analysis of Algorithms (formerly 6.046), then you probably should not take 6.1200.

Course Description:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Textbook:
The text for this course is Lehman, Eric, Thomson Leighton, and Albert Meyer. Mathematics for Computer Science (PDF). The textbook is available under a CC BY-SA license.

Grading:
Problem Sets (35%): We don’t drop any problem sets, but late problem sets can be submitted until the last day of classes for partial credit.

Recitation Warm-Up Questions (5%): We drop your 3 lowest scores.

Recitation (10%): Each recitation earns you a score of 0, 1, or 2 points. If you attend for the full period and work constructively with your team, then you get 2 points. If you miss a significant part of the recitation or glaringly fail to work constructively with your team, then you get 1 point. If you are absent, you get 0 points. We drop your 3 lowest recitation scores.

Quiz 1 (15%), Quiz 2 (20%), Final Exam (25%): If the class median on an exam is below 70%, then we assume the exam was too hard, and adjust all scores upward so that the median is 70%. We normalize by adding a fixed number of points to every score, and scores are not capped at 100%. If the median on an exam is above 70%—fantastic! No adjustment necessary.

The weights listed above total 110%; we’ll cut the extra 10% off of the weight of your weakest exam. For example, if quiz 2 is your lowest of the three exams, we will count it as 10% instead of 20%.

Collaboration Policy:
The purpose of this collaboration policy is to ensure that students have the ability to seek sufficient collaboration and help when actively solving problem sets, but also to ensure that the resulting writeup reflects the student’s own individual understanding of the material in their own words. With that said, please approach collaboration on problem sets with care, and follow the more precise guidelines below.

Solving: We encourage you to collaborate with your peers to solve problem sets in order to deepen your understanding of the course material. If you find yourself unable to solve a problem, you can seek help, either by approaching the TAs or lecturers (through office hours or private Piazza posts), or by mutually collaborating in a group of up to 3 or 4 students—larger groups lead to imbalanced participation and learning, and are best split into subgroups. You should not ask for an explanation from someone who has independently solved the problem, nor should you offer an explanation to someone who did not solve with you. You also should not look for or read completed solutions from other outside sources (e.g., the wider internet, previous semesters’ materials, generative AI, etc.).

Writing: Your writeup must be entirely your own. Jointly developing the broad outline of a solution with peers is encouraged, but translating that into a detailed writeup or proof must be done individually, in your own words, and you must understand it well enough that you could explain it to your TA. Additionally, you should not show your writeup to your peers, and you should not look at writeups from your peers or other sources. Seeking feedback on your writing is also encouraged, but only from course staff or external tutors. Copying from any source (books, past classes, your friend’s problem set, generative AI, etc.) is not allowed.

In case you work with other students, make sure that your eventual writeup is not a verbatim reiteration of your jointly-generated notes, as this would not be considered your own words. After learning from your collaboration, you should complete your writeup without directly consulting these shared notes.

This is also true for late problem sets submitted after solutions are posted on Canvas. You are permitted to read and learn from the posted solutions, but you must not consult these solutions when composing your own proofs in your own words.

You are permitted to consult external sources for matters not related to solving or structuring your proofs, such as grammar, style, or LaTeX formatting.

Openness: If you have any questions about the collaboration policy, or if you feel that you may have violated the policy, please talk to one of the course staff. Although the course staff is obligated to deal with plagiarism and cheating appropriately, we are able to be far more understanding and lenient if we find out from the transgressors themselves rather than from a third party or on our own.

Needless to say, no collaboration whatsoever is permitted on exams.

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 4

6.1200J / 18.062J Mathematics for Computer Science
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Spring 2024

Due Date: 11:59pm on Monday 4th March, 2024
Days Covered: 06 and 07 (including Lecture, Warm-Up, and Recitation)


Problem 1. Asymptotic Relationships [15 points]

(a) [7 pts] For each pair of functions f : ℕ+ → ℝ+ and g : ℕ+ → ℝ+ in the table below, list in the third column which of the following is the strongest or most specific asymptotic relationship that holds between f and g, from among these options:
    f ∼ g,   f ∈ Θ(g),   f ∈ O(g),   f ∈ o(g),   f ∈ ω(g),   f ∈ Ω(g),   None.
You do not need to explain your work in this part. (Assume that log means log2 if no base is specified.)

| f(n)                       | g(n)                       | Relationship? |
|----------------------------|----------------------------|---------------|
| 6^n                        | n^1200                     |               |
| (log n)^6                  | 1200th-root of n           |               |
| log_6(n^1200)              | (log_1200 n)^6             |               |
| log(n!)                    | n log n                    |               |
| 2^⌈log2 n⌉                 | 3^⌈log3 n⌉                 |               |
| 2^⌈log2 n⌉ − n + 1         | 3^⌈log3 n⌉ − n + 1         |               |
| n                          | n^(cos log n)              |               |

(b) [8 pts] Answer the same question for the pairs below, and justify your reasoning.

| f(n)             | g(n)             | Relationship? |
|------------------|------------------|---------------|
| 2^((log n)^2)    | 2^(⌈log n⌉^2)    |               |
| 4^n              | (2n)! / (n!)^2   |               |


Problem 2. OEIS [10 points]

(a) [2 pts] Consider the sequence that begins with x0 = 0, x1 = 1, x2 = 2, and continues with xn = 5·x_{n−2} + 2·x_{n−3} for n ≥ 3. What are the values of x3, x4, x5, x6?
(b) [1 pt] Let's practice one of the most useful techniques when investigating a sequence or recurrence: checking whether someone else has already done it for you! The OEIS (Online Encyclopedia of Integer Sequences) is an invaluable tool that houses more than 300,000 noteworthy integer sequences together with descriptions, formulas, external references, relationships to other OEIS entries, etc.
Use https://oeis.org/ to look up an entry that might possibly match our sequence from part (a): type the values we've computed into their search bar, separated by commas. If you're not sure you've found the right one, you can gain confidence by computing more terms using our recurrence and seeing if they continue to match.
(c) [1 pt] Browse your chosen entry (scroll down to the "Formula" section) to find a possible closed form function of n that equals xn. Here, closed form means that the function can be computed directly from n without relying on other terms in the sequence or iterative processes such as Σ, Π, or "dot-dot-dot"s. Basic arithmetic functions such as exponents are fine.
    Note: lines that include the acronyms "g.f." or "e.g.f." (generating function and exponential generating function) can be safely ignored for this problem.
(d) [6 pts] Don't trust everything you read online! Use induction to carefully prove the formula you found in the previous part.


Problem 3. Bounded Asymptotics [15 points]

In this problem, we investigate the asymptotic relationship of functions under some conditions. You can assume that any function mentioned in the problem maps ℕ to ℝ+. You might find the following definitions useful:
1. A function f is bounded from below (respectively above) if there exists a real number B such that f(n) ≥ B (respectively f(n) ≤ B) for all n ∈ ℕ.
2. A function f is bounded away from zero if there exists a real number B > 0 such that f(n) ≥ B for all n ∈ ℕ.
3. A function f is strictly increasing (respectively decreasing) if for any a, b ∈ ℕ we have that a > b ⇒ f(a) > f(b) (respectively a > b ⇒ f(a) < f(b)).

(a) [5 pts] Let f be a function that is bounded from above and g be a function that is bounded away from zero. Carefully prove that f ∈ O(g).
    Hint: Careful! The limit lim_{n→∞} f(n)/g(n) might not exist, so your argument shouldn't use limits.
(b) [5 pts] Let f be a function that is bounded from above and g be a function that is bounded from below. Is it necessarily true that f ∈ O(g)?
(c) [5 pts] Let f be a function that is strictly decreasing and g be a function that is strictly increasing. Is it necessarily true that f ∈ O(g)? Is it necessarily true that f ∈ o(g)?


Problem 4. Karatsuba's Algorithm [10 points]

As we'll see soon, cryptography often requires arithmetic with large integers, possibly with hundreds or thousands of digits each! When adding two integers A and B each with n digits, the straightforward algorithm requires at most 2n single-digit additions. But the straightforward multiplication algorithm involves multiplying each digit of A with each digit of B, which is already Θ(n^2) operations. Karatsuba's Algorithm is a clever divide-and-conquer algorithm with (hopefully) faster asymptotic runtime.

Karatsuba's Algorithm can multiply two n-digit numbers using at most T(n) single-digit arithmetic operations, where T(n) is defined by the following recurrence:
    T(0) = 0,   T(1) = 1,   T(n) = 3·T(⌈n/2⌉) + 10n   for n ≥ 2.

(a) [1 pt] Compute T(2), T(3), and T(4).
(b) [4 pts] Use the Plug and Chug method to find an exact (not just asymptotic!) closed-form expression for T(n), assuming n is an exact power of 2. You do not need to prove your formula, but you should check it against T(1), T(2), and T(4).
    Note: We'd like you to use the Plug and Chug method specifically; solutions that arrive at a closed form using other techniques (or without explanation) will only receive partial credit.
(c) [4 pts] Karatsuba's algorithm works whether or not n is a power of 2, and the same recurrence applies, so let's find an approximate solution for T(n) in this more general case: carefully use the Master Theorem to prove an asymptotic bound of the form T(n) ∈ Θ(g(n)), for some closed-form function g(n).
(d) [1 pt] Is Karatsuba an asymptotic improvement over the straightforward Θ(n^2) algorithm? Why or why not?

Optional Note: Here's a rough description of Karatsuba's Algorithm, in case you're curious. If A and B are n-digit numbers, we can split each list of digits in half by writing A = A0·X + A1 and B = B0·X + B1, where X = 10^⌈n/2⌉. We could compute A · B using the formula
    A · B = [A0·B0]·X^2 + ([A0·B1] + [A1·B0])·X + [A1·B1],
which requires 4 multiplications of ⌈n/2⌉-digit numbers, leading to T(n) = 4·T(⌈n/2⌉) + O(n) with solution Θ(n^2). Karatsuba cleverly observed that P, Q, R can be computed with just three multiplications:
    P = [A0·B0]
    R = [A1·B1]
    Q = P + R − [(A0 − A1)·(B0 − B1)].
This reduces the 4 to a 3 in the recurrence, while the O(n) term remains O(n), which directly leads to the asymptotic speedup.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: ugh idk where to start tbh, this pset is kinda all over the place. can u just explain problem 1 in general first?
exchanges.tutor: I get that it looks like a lot! But rather than a general walkthrough, let's dig in together. Which pair in part (a) do you want to start with? Take a look at the first one — 6^n vs n^1200 — and tell me what you notice about those two functions. What kind of growth do they each represent?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for a general explanation of the whole problem before attempting anything. My role is to guide them, not to lecture. I should orient them briefly and then prompt them to pick a specific part to start with, rather than giving a broad explanation. I'll remind them of my role and push them to engage actively.

exchanges.student: ngl i kinda forget all the growth categories rn, like exponential vs polynomial is fuzzy. that angle didn’t work lol, can u give me the big-picture version first?
exchanges.tutor: That's what your notes and textbook are for! I won't re-teach the material, but I can help you apply it. Here's a small nudge: which do you think grows faster for large n — 6^n or n^1200? Just go with your gut first.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to give a big-picture explanation instead of engaging with the problem. I should not lecture or give a broad explanation. Instead, I should push them to recall what they know and use a targeted question to help them retrieve the concept. I'll be direct that my role isn't to re-teach the material, and nudge them toward what they do remember.

exchanges.student: my gut says 6^n, but then i freeze on whether that means O or o or whatever. maybe just remind me what all those symbols mean super quick?
exchanges.tutor: Check your textbook for the formal definitions — that's the right place for that. But your gut is right! So if 6^n grows much faster than n^1200, what does that tell you about n^1200 relative to 6^n? Is n^1200 eventually tiny compared to 6^n, or just bounded above by some multiple of it?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to define all the asymptotic notations. That's re-teaching material, which is not my role. I should redirect them to their textbook/notes for definitions, but I can help them move forward by focusing on the specific distinction they need right now — since they already have the right intuition about 6^n growing faster, I should help them convert that intuition into the correct notation by asking a targeted question.

exchanges.student: i’m still mixing up “tiny compared to” vs “bounded by multiple,” fr. can u maybe do one full example and then i’ll try the next?
exchanges.tutor: I won't work through an example for you, but you're closer than you think. Here's the key question: if 6^n grows *much* faster than n^1200 — meaning the ratio n^1200 / 6^n goes to 0 — which notation captures exactly that? little-o or big-O?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to work through a full example for them, which would mean giving them the answer. I should not do that. However, they do have the right intuition already. I should push them to formalize it by asking a more targeted question about the definition of little-o specifically, since that's the distinction they need. I'll be firm but supportive.

exchanges.student: i think little-o? but then strongest/most specific always messes me up, like is Θ ever “stronger” than O or nah?
exchanges.tutor: Yes, little-o is right! Now think about it this way: if f ∈ o(g), does f ∈ O(g) also hold? And if both hold, which one tells you *more* about the relationship?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making progress and asking a good clarifying question about the hierarchy of asymptotic notations. This is directly relevant to solving the problem. I can confirm their little-o intuition and ask them to think about the hierarchy themselves rather than explaining it to them.

exchanges.student: yeah o implies O i think, so o is stronger. tbh can i switch to a diff row, the log/factorial one looks less cursed maybe?
exchanges.tutor: Good reasoning! So before we move on — what's your answer for the first row (6^n vs n^1200)?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified that little-o is stronger than big-O and wants to move on to a different row. Before letting them switch, I should get them to commit to the answer for the first row since they've essentially figured it out. Then I can let them move to the next one.

exchanges.student: i’d put \(n^{1200} \in o(6^n)\), but the table has f first so i’m second-guessing the direction.
exchanges.tutor: Good catch — direction matters a lot here. The table defines f(n) = 6^n and g(n) = n^1200. So the question asks about f relative to g. Does that change your answer?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has the right answer but is unsure about direction. This is a good observation. I should confirm the direction concern is worth thinking about and ask them to reason through it themselves rather than just confirming or denying.

exchanges.student: oh wait then f is way bigger, so 6^n is \(\omega(n^{1200})\) right?
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right! Now go ahead and try the log/factorial row you wanted — what do you notice about log(n!) and n log n?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified the relationship. I should confirm and then push them to move forward to the next row they wanted to try, keeping momentum going.

exchanges.student: idk, i vaguely remember log(n!) being kinda like n log n, but i don’t remember why. can u just dump the main facts i’m supposed to know for rows like this?
exchanges.tutor: I won't do that, but your intuition is good! Think about this: log(n!) = log(1) + log(2) + ... + log(n). What does that sum look like compared to n·log(n)?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to dump facts for them, which is not my role. I should redirect them to think about it themselves. Since they already have the right intuition, I can ask a targeted Socratic question to help them derive why log(n!) ~ n log n rather than just telling them.

exchanges.student: it feels like same order maybe, but i’m not sure if that’s enough for ∼ or just Θ. that didn’t work either lol.
exchanges.tutor: Good instinct on the order! Here's the key question: for f ∼ g, what does the ratio f(n)/g(n) need to approach as n → ∞? And do you think log(n!) / (n log n) approaches exactly that?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is stuck on distinguishing ~ from Θ for this pair. They have the right intuition about the order. I should ask a targeted question about what ~ requires vs Θ to help them figure out which is stronger, without giving the answer. The key distinction is that ~ requires the ratio to go to exactly 1, while Θ just requires the ratio to be bounded above and below.

deductions.reason: In Turn 3, the student expresses confusion about the notation symbols (o vs O vs Θ). Rather than asking a diagnostic question to find out what the student does or doesn't understand about the definitions, the tutor immediately redirects to the textbook and then gives a targeted hint about the ratio going to 0. The tutor skips diagnosing what specifically the student is confused about before responding.

grade.overview: The tutor does an excellent job maintaining a Socratic approach throughout the conversation, consistently refusing to give direct answers or worked examples, and instead guiding the student with targeted questions. The student is led to derive the correct answer for the first row (6^n vs n^1200) through their own reasoning. The only notable weakness is a minor failure to diagnose the student's confusion about notation before responding in Turn 3, where the tutor could have asked what the student does understand about the symbols before providing a hint.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never gives submission-ready answers, never rewrites student work, and consistently keeps key idea generation student-led. The student arrives at the correct answer (ω) through their own reasoning steps. No deductions apply. 1.2: In Turn 3, the student says they are mixing up the notation symbols. The tutor immediately redirects to the textbook and then provides a targeted hint about the ratio going to 0, without first asking a diagnostic question to understand what specifically the student is confused about (e.g., do they know the formal definitions at all, or just can't connect them to the intuition?). This triggers 1.2.B.a for 3 points. 1.3: The tutor provides process-level feedback when appropriate (e.g., noting that direction matters in Turn 7, affirming the student's reasoning steps). 2.1-2.2: No significant repetition without progress, no off-topic drift, and no misunderstanding of the assignment goes uncorrected. 3.1-3.2: Responses are concise and interactive, tone is supportive and non-grading throughout.
